evidence_quotes.quote: In-Game Currency Is Not a Financial Instrument Request: Do not classify in-game currencies as virtual/financial currencies or digital representations of value.
Price Indication & Multi-Currency Systems Support: transparent pre-contract information, no drip pricing, and optional real-currency display.
Uncertainty-Based Rewards (Loot Boxes) Support: proportionate, age-appropriate safeguardsprobability disclosures, cooldowns, and parental platform-level gating.
Pay-to-Progress / Pay-to-Win Features Support: transparency and no misleading claims.
Minors Protection & Parental Empowerment Support: default-on controls (spend limits, approvals, playtime settings) and age-appropriate design, implemented via platform-level standards.

paragraphs.text: EXECUTIVE SUMMARY Several current DFA options risk mischaracterising how video games work, duplicating or conflicting with existing EU and national law, and unintentionally harming European consumers, developers, and the wider creative economy. A balanced approach is neededone that maintains player protection and market integrity without redefining in-game currencies as financial instruments, banning common game features, or layering overlapping rules on top of effective frameworks already in force (UCPD, CRD/Omnibus, GDPR, DSA, CPC network actions). The Commission should preserve what worksstrong consumer law and coordinated CPC enforcementwhile issuing targeted, evidence-based guidance to close actual gaps. Redefining in-game currencies as money, banning legitimate mechanics, or duplicating existing law would harm players and Europes games sector without delivering better protection. 1) In-Game Currency Is Not a Financial Instrument Request: Do not classify in-game currencies as virtual/financial currencies or digital representations of value. Constructive alternative: Clarify that in-game currencies are digital content tokens, redeemable only within the relevant game/service and governed by digital content consumer law (fit-for-purpose, withdrawal rights, etc.), not by financial services rules. 2) Build on Existing Strong FrameworksAvoid Duplication Request: Rely on and coordinate existing law and enforcement, rather than creating new, overlapping regimes. Keep enforcement risk-based and evidence-drivennot categorical. 3) Protect Europes Creative Economy and Player Experience Request: Conduct a full impact assessment on jobs, SMEs, and player welfare before introducing measures that restrict normal monetisation models. Why it matters: The video games industry is a major digital employer and cultural driver across Europe. Overly broad rules that invalidate established monetisation tools would cut revenues, stifle updates and live services, and cause job losseshurting players as well as developers. As one of the European video gaming companies we would like to warn that some DFA options could massively damage the sectorraising prices, eliminating free-to-play access, and reducing content support. These concerns deserve serious consideration in the Impact Assessment. Specific Positions on Consultation Themes A. Price Indication & Multi-Currency Systems Support: transparent pre-contract information, no drip pricing, and optional real-currency display. Oppose: bans on in-game currencies or mandatory EUR-only pricing, which would break cross-platform ecosystems and penalise loyal players. B. Uncertainty-Based Rewards (Loot Boxes) Support: proportionate, age-appropriate safeguardsprobability disclosures, cooldowns, and parental platform-level gating. Oppose: blanket bans without strong evidence of harm. C. Pay-to-Progress / Pay-to-Win Features Support: transparency and no misleading claims. Oppose: categorical bans that remove accessibility options or undermine free-to-play models. D. Minors Protection & Parental Empowerment Support: default-on controls (spend limits, approvals, playtime settings) and age-appropriate design, implemented via platform-level standards. Use CPC network action plans and UCPD/CRD guidance for fast, clear enforcement. Avoid overlapping or inconsistent new regimes that confuse compliance. CONCLUSION: The DFA should enhancenot replaceEuropes proven consumer protection system. By keeping rules proportionate, evidence-driven, and consistent with existing law, the EU can safeguard players, ensure fair play, and sustain Europes thriving creative economy. Contact: FunPlus International AG, Bahnhofstrasse 2, 6300 Zug, Switzerland, email: GCoffice@funplus.com
